title: Bronchiolitis — Assessment
oneLiner: Acute viral lower respiratory tract infection in infants <12 months; leads to bronchiolar inflammation & respiratory distress—most common cause of winter hospitalisation in this age group.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Respiratory failure with impending apnoea** — single finding: *grunting, head-bobbing, or prolonged apnoea (>20 sec) despite oxygen* → this is not a “mild” case; escalate to HDU/ICU immediately.
- **Sepsis / bacterial superinfection** — single finding: *high fever (>39°C) with toxic appearance and focal consolidation on CXR* → treat with IV antibiotics *after* blood cultures, do not wait for CRP.
- **Underlying congenital heart disease (CHD) unmasked** — single finding: *cyanosis that persists despite corrected hypoxia on oxygen* or *failure to improve with standard therapy* → urgent echocardiogram; call cardiology/ICU.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Coryzal prodrome (rhinorrhoea, mild fever)
  - Persistent cough (often paroxysmal)
  - Tachypnoea, difficulty feeding (poor intake, decreased wet nappies)
  - In very young infants (<3 months): apnoeic episodes without preceding cough
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachypnoea (age-specific centiles)
  - Subcostal / intercostal retractions, nasal flaring
  - Auscultation: fine inspiratory crackles (crepitations) ± high-pitched wheeze
  - Oxygen saturations (room air): <92% = desaturation (NICE threshold for oxygen)
  - Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, prolonged capillary refill, sunken fontanelle (in infants)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral pneumonia** → focal crackles, persistent high fever, CXR shows lobar consolidation; *splitter:* bronchiolitis has bilateral wheeze/crackles without focal signs.
- **Asthma (in older infants >12 months)** → recurrent wheeze, family history, bronchodilator response; *splitter:* bronchiolitis rarely responds to salbutamol.
- **Foreign body aspiration** → sudden onset, asymmetric breath sounds, not coryzal; *splitter: no prodrome + unilateral wheeze.*
- **Pertussis** → paroxysmal cough with inspiratory whoop, no wheeze, no crackles; *splitter: cough after coryzal phase without respiratory distress.*
- **Congenital heart disease / heart failure** → hepatomegaly, gallop rhythm, cyanosis; *splitter: no response to oxygen, cardiomegaly on CXR.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Oxygen saturations (continuous monitoring)
  - Respiratory rate (full minute count), heart rate, capillary refill
  - Work of breathing assessment (Silverman-Andersen score or NICE severity tool)
  - Apnoea monitoring (if <6 months or <3 months)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, blood culture (if fever >38.5°C or toxic)
  - U&E (if clinically dehydrated or on IV fluids)
  - Capillary gas (if moderate-severe respiratory distress to check PCO₂)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR only if atypical presentation (focal signs, no prodrome, severe hypoxia) or to rule out complications (pneumothorax, effusion, lobar collapse)
  - **Second-line:** no indication for CT or routine ultrasound
- **Special tests:**
  - Nasopharyngeal aspirate for viral PCR (RSV, rhinovirus, adenovirus) – aids cohorting / infection control, but does not change acute management
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE guideline [NG9]: clinical diagnosis (coryza + cough + tachypnoea + bilateral wheeze/crackles in infant <12 months); severity scoring based on respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, feeding, apnoea
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **CXR** if infant fails to improve after 48 hours of supportive care OR has unilateral signs → rule out secondary bacterial pneumonia / pneumothorax
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Oxygen** via nasal cannula / headbox to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 92% (if desaturation)
  - **Nasal suction** (gentle bulb or catheter) to clear secretions before feeds – improves work of breathing
  - **Trial of feed** – if mild-moderate and able to suck safely, continue oral feeds (small, frequent); if moderate-severe, hold oral and start NG/IV
  - **IV fluids** if unable to feed (dehydration) or severe distress (RR >60, SpO₂ <92%)
  - **CPAP** (nasal) if moderate-severe respiratory distress (grunting, retractions with hypercapnia) – escalate to senior
- **Ward (first 24-72 hours):**
  - Continue oxygen weaning (target SpO₂ ≥92%)
  - Monitor work of breathing, saturations, feeding tolerance every 1-2 hours
  - NG feeding if oral not safe (but try to keep enteral to avoid IV)
  - Regular suction as needed (not routine)
  - Consider physiotherapy for sticky secretions (only if lobar collapse)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - No specific therapy; recovery over 7-14 days
  - Palivizumab prophylaxis only for high-risk infants (extreme prematurity, CHD) – not used acutely
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose (0.45% NaCl for term infants >1 month) – rate: maintenance (check local policy; e.g., 100 mL/kg/day in first 10 kg)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not indicated (infants have low risk)
  - **Diet:** NBM if moderate-severe (NG or IV); allow oral feeds when safe
  - **Observation:** respiratory rate, SpO₂, heart rate, work of breathing Q1–2h; PEWS score Q4h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oxygen** – humidified as needed; aim SpO₂ ≥ 92%
  - **Salbutamol** – NOT routinely recommended (NICE: no evidence) – only trial if strong asthma phenotype (e.g., older infant, prior wheeze); dose: [check local policy] via MDI + spacer
  - **Antibiotics** – do NOT give routinely; start only if confirmed bacterial co-infection (fever >39°C, focal CXR, high CRP) → e.g., co-amoxiclav 30 mg/kg TDS oral/IV (dose per BNFc)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Adrenaline (nebulised)** – sometimes trialled in severe upper airway obstruction (croup-like) but not standard; [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bronchodilators may cause tachycardia, worsen hypoxia if overused – use sparingly
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doses of IV fluids are weight-based; no specific drug adjustments in bronchiolitis
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Fluid overload** – calculating maintenance incorrectly (e.g., using adult formula) leads to hyponatraemic seizures; always use paediatric maintenance (e.g., 100 mL/kg/day for first 10 kg)
  - **Oxygen without monitoring** – hypoxic drive in infants is fragile; wean slowly
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure with hypercapnia (PCO₂ >60 mmHg = impending exhaustion)
  - Apnoea (especially in preterm or <6 months)
  - Dehydration / electrolyte disturbance
  - Secondary bacterial pneumonia (often pneumococcal or H. influenzae)
  - Pneumothorax (rare, from vigorous CPAP or severe coughing)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-bronchiolitis wheeze / increased asthma risk (in some children)
  - No chronic lung damage in healthy infants
sections.Prognosis: - Majority recover completely within 1-2 weeks; mortality <1% in term infants. Risk factors for severe outcome: prematurity, CHD, chronic lung disease (BPD), immunodeficiency.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always check hydration status (wet nappies, fontanelle, mucous membranes) before deciding between NG and IV – NG preserves gut and reduces IV risks.
- Use a structured severity score (e.g., NICE or PEWS) to track deterioration objectively.
- In infants <3 months with apnoea, admit even if saturations are normal – apnoea can precede severe desaturation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do not routinely request CXR – it rarely changes management, exposes infants to radiation, and may show atelectasis mistaken for pneumonia.
- Do not start antibiotics unless strong suspicion of bacterial co-infection – prevents resistance and unnecessary side effects.
- Avoid using bronchodilators as a “trial” – NICE says no evidence; can cause tachycardia and worsen hypoxia.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening work of breathing (rib recession, grunting, head-bobbing)
  - Unable to feed (taking <50% of usual intake) or reduced wet nappies (<4 in 24 hours)
  - Desaturation episodes or apnoea (stopping breathing for >10 seconds)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48-72 hours if mild; no specialist follow-up needed unless complex (e.g., prematurity, CHD)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Increasing oxygen requirement (>28% FiO₂) or SpO₂ <90% despite moderate oxygen
  - Rising PCO₂ (gas >55 mmHg) or falling pH (<7.25)
  - Persistent apnoea (>20 sec) or bradycardia
  - PEWS ≥ 4 or clinical deterioration
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Need for CPAP/BIPAP or impending intubation (exhaustion, apnoea, PCO₂ >60)
  - Shock (tachycardia, poor perfusion, hypotension)
  - GCS <15 (hypoxic encephalopathy)

rawMarkdown:
## Bronchiolitis — Assessment

**One-liner:** Acute viral lower respiratory tract infection in infants <12 months; leads to bronchiolar inflammation & respiratory distress—most common cause of winter hospitalisation in this age group.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Respiratory failure with impending apnoea** — single finding: *grunting, head-bobbing, or prolonged apnoea (>20 sec) despite oxygen* → this is not a “mild” case; escalate to HDU/ICU immediately.
- **Sepsis / bacterial superinfection** — single finding: *high fever (>39°C) with toxic appearance and focal consolidation on CXR* → treat with IV antibiotics *after* blood cultures, do not wait for CRP.
- **Underlying congenital heart disease (CHD) unmasked** — single finding: *cyanosis that persists despite corrected hypoxia on oxygen* or *failure to improve with standard therapy* → urgent echocardiogram; call cardiology/ICU.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Coryzal prodrome (rhinorrhoea, mild fever)
  - Persistent cough (often paroxysmal)
  - Tachypnoea, difficulty feeding (poor intake, decreased wet nappies)
  - In very young infants (<3 months): apnoeic episodes without preceding cough
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Tachypnoea (age-specific centiles)
  - Subcostal / intercostal retractions, nasal flaring
  - Auscultation: fine inspiratory crackles (crepitations) ± high-pitched wheeze
  - Oxygen saturations (room air): <92% = desaturation (NICE threshold for oxygen)
  - Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, prolonged capillary refill, sunken fontanelle (in infants)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral pneumonia** → focal crackles, persistent high fever, CXR shows lobar consolidation; *splitter:* bronchiolitis has bilateral wheeze/crackles without focal signs.
- **Asthma (in older infants >12 months)** → recurrent wheeze, family history, bronchodilator response; *splitter:* bronchiolitis rarely responds to salbutamol.
- **Foreign body aspiration** → sudden onset, asymmetric breath sounds, not coryzal; *splitter: no prodrome + unilateral wheeze.*
- **Pertussis** → paroxysmal cough with inspiratory whoop, no wheeze, no crackles; *splitter: cough after coryzal phase without respiratory distress.*
- **Congenital heart disease / heart failure** → hepatomegaly, gallop rhythm, cyanosis; *splitter: no response to oxygen, cardiomegaly on CXR.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Oxygen saturations (continuous monitoring)
  - Respiratory rate (full minute count), heart rate, capillary refill
  - Work of breathing assessment (Silverman-Andersen score or NICE severity tool)
  - Apnoea monitoring (if <6 months or <3 months)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, blood culture (if fever >38.5°C or toxic)
  - U&E (if clinically dehydrated or on IV fluids)
  - Capillary gas (if moderate-severe respiratory distress to check PCO₂)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR only if atypical presentation (focal signs, no prodrome, severe hypoxia) or to rule out complications (pneumothorax, effusion, lobar collapse)
  - **Second-line:** no indication for CT or routine ultrasound
- **Special tests:**
  - Nasopharyngeal aspirate for viral PCR (RSV, rhinovirus, adenovirus) – aids cohorting / infection control, but does not change acute management
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE guideline [NG9]: clinical diagnosis (coryza + cough + tachypnoea + bilateral wheeze/crackles in infant <12 months); severity scoring based on respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, feeding, apnoea
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **CXR** if infant fails to improve after 48 hours of supportive care OR has unilateral signs → rule out secondary bacterial pneumonia / pneumothorax

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Oxygen** via nasal cannula / headbox to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 92% (if desaturation)
  - **Nasal suction** (gentle bulb or catheter) to clear secretions before feeds – improves work of breathing
  - **Trial of feed** – if mild-moderate and able to suck safely, continue oral feeds (small, frequent); if moderate-severe, hold oral and start NG/IV
  - **IV fluids** if unable to feed (dehydration) or severe distress (RR >60, SpO₂ <92%)
  - **CPAP** (nasal) if moderate-severe respiratory distress (grunting, retractions with hypercapnia) – escalate to senior
- **Ward (first 24-72 hours):**
  - Continue oxygen weaning (target SpO₂ ≥92%)
  - Monitor work of breathing, saturations, feeding tolerance every 1-2 hours
  - NG feeding if oral not safe (but try to keep enteral to avoid IV)
  - Regular suction as needed (not routine)
  - Consider physiotherapy for sticky secretions (only if lobar collapse)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - No specific therapy; recovery over 7-14 days
  - Palivizumab prophylaxis only for high-risk infants (extreme prematurity, CHD) – not used acutely
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose (0.45% NaCl for term infants >1 month) – rate: maintenance (check local policy; e.g., 100 mL/kg/day in first 10 kg)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not indicated (infants have low risk)
  - **Diet:** NBM if moderate-severe (NG or IV); allow oral feeds when safe
  - **Observation:** respiratory rate, SpO₂, heart rate, work of breathing Q1–2h; PEWS score Q4h

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oxygen** – humidified as needed; aim SpO₂ ≥ 92%
  - **Salbutamol** – NOT routinely recommended (NICE: no evidence) – only trial if strong asthma phenotype (e.g., older infant, prior wheeze); dose: [check local policy] via MDI + spacer
  - **Antibiotics** – do NOT give routinely; start only if confirmed bacterial co-infection (fever >39°C, focal CXR, high CRP) → e.g., co-amoxiclav 30 mg/kg TDS oral/IV (dose per BNFc)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Adrenaline (nebulised)** – sometimes trialled in severe upper airway obstruction (croup-like) but not standard; [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bronchodilators may cause tachycardia, worsen hypoxia if overused – use sparingly
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doses of IV fluids are weight-based; no specific drug adjustments in bronchiolitis
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Fluid overload** – calculating maintenance incorrectly (e.g., using adult formula) leads to hyponatraemic seizures; always use paediatric maintenance (e.g., 100 mL/kg/day for first 10 kg)
  - **Oxygen without monitoring** – hypoxic drive in infants is fragile; wean slowly

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure with hypercapnia (PCO₂ >60 mmHg = impending exhaustion)
  - Apnoea (especially in preterm or <6 months)
  - Dehydration / electrolyte disturbance
  - Secondary bacterial pneumonia (often pneumococcal or H. influenzae)
  - Pneumothorax (rare, from vigorous CPAP or severe coughing)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-bronchiolitis wheeze / increased asthma risk (in some children)
  - No chronic lung damage in healthy infants

### Prognosis
- Majority recover completely within 1-2 weeks; mortality <1% in term infants. Risk factors for severe outcome: prematurity, CHD, chronic lung disease (BPD), immunodeficiency.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always check hydration status (wet nappies, fontanelle, mucous membranes) before deciding between NG and IV – NG preserves gut and reduces IV risks.
- Use a structured severity score (e.g., NICE or PEWS) to track deterioration objectively.
- In infants <3 months with apnoea, admit even if saturations are normal – apnoea can precede severe desaturation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do not routinely request CXR – it rarely changes management, exposes infants to radiation, and may show atelectasis mistaken for pneumonia.
- Do not start antibiotics unless strong suspicion of bacterial co-infection – prevents resistance and unnecessary side effects.
- Avoid using bronchodilators as a “trial” – NICE says no evidence; can cause tachycardia and worsen hypoxia.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening work of breathing (rib recession, grunting, head-bobbing)
  - Unable to feed (taking <50% of usual intake) or reduced wet nappies (<4 in 24 hours)
  - Desaturation episodes or apnoea (stopping breathing for >10 seconds)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48-72 hours if mild; no specialist follow-up needed unless complex (e.g., prematurity, CHD)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Increasing oxygen requirement (>28% FiO₂) or SpO₂ <90% despite moderate oxygen
  - Rising PCO₂ (gas >55 mmHg) or falling pH (<7.25)
  - Persistent apnoea (>20 sec) or bradycardia
  - PEWS ≥ 4 or clinical deterioration
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Need for CPAP/BIPAP or impending intubation (exhaustion, apnoea, PCO₂ >60)
  - Shock (tachycardia, poor perfusion, hypotension)
  - GCS <15 (hypoxic encephalopathy)
